paragraphs.text: TRANSACTION SERVICES WORKPLAN
The scope of work will cover the following work streams and will be led by an experienced, multidisciplinary team.
The initial scope and fee estimate may be refined further as additional information is received.
Financial due diligence ($400,000 - $500,000)
Operations due diligence – Phase I
Separation assessment ($150,000 - $200,000)
Operations due diligence – Phase II
Post Close PMO Support ($270,000 - $370,000)
Financial Due Diligence
Unless otherwise noted, our work will concentrate on the last two fiscal years ended March 31, 2022, 2023 and latest available periods (i.e. trailing twelve month November/December 2023).
Understanding Target’s earnings sustainability and momentum and business footprint
Sustainability/Quality of Earnings
Read and comment on the adjusted EBITDA prepared by Target and its sell-side advisors, and:
Roll-forward the quality of earnings analysis included in the sell-side diligence report accordingly;
Propose any additional potential adjustments to historical earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (“EBITDA”), presented on a consolidated basis, by considering:

The validity / impact of adjustments proposed by Management and the sell-side due diligence advisors, including Restructuring costs; Impact of loyalty program breakage; Sourcing changes; Vacation policy change; Accounting changes; Other normalization adjustments; Pro forma and run-rate adjustments; Carve-out, stand alone, corporate allocation considerations.
Work with Client to consider synergy considerations, purchasing rebates, etc.(See below synergies validation for additional detail);
Other large, unusual or non-recurring events or transactions that may have distorted results;
Other potential items discovered in the due diligence process.
Supporting analyses to Sustainability / Quality of Earnings
Store portfolio analysis

Obtain store P&L data to perform analysis to understand the drivers of historical sales and profitability development within the store portfolio to perform the following (to the extent possible based on information availability and transaction timelines):
Sales and profitability by store location (including same store sales analysis, front store vs pharmacy, cost and margin profiles);
Segment the store portfolio to assess historical performance based on the following:
Outlier performance – top quartile vs bottom quartile performing stores
Store-type – Suburban / Dense Urban / Rural / etc., size format, storefront vs mall, etc
Existing (same) stores, new stores, renovated stores, closed stores; standard, specialty, next gen
Cohort analysis based on vintage year:
Ramp analysis to understand ramp profile and assess reasonability of pro forma revenue and EBITDA maturity adjustments for recently opened locations;
Store productivity and KPI metrics (e.g. Rx count, Rx revenue per Rx, sales/profitability per square foot);
Revenue and margin analysis

Understands the health and sustainability of revenue and margin, that will bring clarity and insights into the drivers of business performance to strengthen deal conviction. Provides confidence in value creation planning / realization by uncovering opportunities earlier.
Obtain and read an analysis of Target’s revenue and inquire about:

Leveraging KPMG’s proprietary SPI (strategic profitability insights) tool, use the transaction / sales database from the IT / ERP systems to analyze revenue and profitability (cost, price, margin), key KPIs (e.g., average sales price, average order size, etc.) and performance trends (e.g., concentration, price-volume-mix, high growth SKUs, negative margins, etc.); evaluating by front-store and Pharmacy (Rx), Category (Dispensary, Over-the-Counter Products, Health and Beauty, Home Healthcare, Grocery, etc.), sub-category (Rexall - Chronic, Acute, Services, Specialty, etc.; Well.ca – Food & Snacks, Vitamins, Beauty, etc.), channel (brick & mortar, eCom), by brand (owned - Rexall, Kit, Nosh & Co, etc. or national), and by geography (store ID, postal code, city, etc.)
Where information is available, also include Front of Store Partners (e.g., DAVIDsTea, Staples, etc.) and eCom Partners (Doordash, Well.ca, Uber Eats, etc.)
Where information is available, generic versus branded drugs
Where information is available, public, private, or cash

Integrate anonymized customer data to understand revenue and mix (one-time versus recurring) across different locations; use of BeWell loyalty programs (e.g., impact of discounts, recurring nature of customers, etc.); and how this impacts overall revenue sustainability
Store costs analysed as a percentage of sales or applicable metric (selling payroll, other store payroll, store rent, marketing, shrinkage, sales-by-square footage, etc.).
Sales and COGS adjustments (including sales returns, markdowns, supplier revenue, stock shrinkage, obsolescence, other income, loyalty points and gift cards);
Consolidate the data into a clean Excel data cube and PowerBI Analysis file, and extract and understand any patterns and trends that might inform value creation and optimization opportunities
Operating expenses
Obtain and read an analysis of the Target’s selling, general and administration expenses and inquire about:
Salaries and wages (including headcount by area and function, if possible);
General and administrative expenses;
Information systems expenses;
Corporate costs and any allocations to store level as applicable; clarify expenses with McKesson
Extraordinary expenses;
Rent and leases; and
Miscellaneous and other expenses.
Understanding Target’s net working capital, cash conversion cycle and cash flow profile
Working capital
Summarize historical working capital in the business including:
The composition of individual working capital accounts (e.g. trade receivables, inventory, accounts payable and accruals, etc.);
Working capital trends and impact on reported metrics;
Large, unusual or non-operating items that may have affected normal working capital trends, (such as accruals for bonuses, understanding non-trade balances, etc.);
Understanding the accounting impact of allowance for uncollectible amounts, provisions, and other accruals requiring judgment;
Working capital seasonality; and
Historical trends in key working capital metrics.
Supporting analyses to working capital
Inventory
Obtain and read an analysis of Target’s inventory and inquire and comment on:
The inventory valuation methodology (e.g. standard costing);
Significant vendor allowances, rebates, allowances and/or purchase commitments and related programs;
Return to vendor provisions;
Inventory aging and estimated provision, if any (i.e. slow-moving inventory).
Accounts receivable
Obtain and read an analysis of Target’s accounts receivable and inquire about:
Aging analysis and related credit terms (including special terms);
Trade and non-trade balances;
Allowance for uncollectible accounts and write-offs; and
Reserve and adjustments.
Other assets (i.e. prepaid accounts)
Obtain a summary of and comment on unusual items, significant fluctuations, and significant balances.
Accounts payable and accruals
Obtain and read an analysis of Target’s accounts payable and accrued expenses and inquire about:
AP aging analysis, terms of trade with major vendors and days payables outstanding;
Accrued expenses; and
Other current and non-current liabilities.
Debt and debt-like items

Prepare a summary of debt and debt-like items (e.g. notes payable, capital vendor payables, deferred purchase price of property or services, accrued bonuses, capital lease obligation, etc.), commitments and contingencies.
Capital expenditures and fixed assets
Obtain and read an analysis of historical and future capital cost requirements including:
Fixed asset continuity schedules (including significant additions and disposals);
Maintenance versus growth capital expenditures; and
Planned, committed, and deferred capital expenditures.
Understanding Target’s accounting and financial reporting environment
Target’s accounting profile
Read Target’s financial statements and discuss them with management to gain an understanding of accounting policies and practices including:

Finance function, financial reporting framework, and management reporting relationships (including process, timing, structure of financial reporting, and interaction with the information/accounting systems);
Significant accounting estimates (i.e. allowances for doubtful accounts, inventory provisions, accruals, etc.);
Recent or contemplated changes in accounting principles, procedures, or estimates;
Intercompany accounts and related party transactions, if any.
Audit working papers (if required)
Obtain and read the auditor’s working papers for the latest fiscal year:
Comment on the nature and volume of audit differences (recorded and unrecorded adjustments), use of accounting estimates, any changes in accounting policies or methods; and
Comment on control issues identified by the external auditors, if applicable.
Comment on any additional "red flags" or key risks as identified in the provided audit materials
Review of the audit committee reporting and presentations from the audit firm
Other
Assistance with transaction documentation (if required)
Assist with relevant asset purchase agreement financial definitions (e.g. net working capital and debt-like item definitions and adjustment procedures).
Operational Diligence & Support
Phase 1 - Separation assessment diligence (3-4 weeks)

The objective is to provide an assessment of the separation considerations in this transaction and estimate the required incremental on-going and one-time costs for the Target to operate independently from the Seller. Our primary focus is to provide a robust view of the cost considerations of the carve-out business with separation considerations. This will ensure we get a view of the whole business as a standalone and set a baseline in order to integrate into SDM. Key entanglements include Supply, IT systems (propel, auto star), Corporate recharges, and others that will require diligence.
Documents Review

Review any deal related documents (ex. CIM, models, plans, TSAs, Purchase Agreements, ancillary agreements, etc.) that may exist. Where appropriate, we will provide commentary on areas for negotiation and guidance on market terms in order to protect your interests and best position you for integration readiness.
Separation Perimeters

Based on the information available, document and summarize the key areas of entanglement between the Target and Parent, indicating those elements that would transfer with the business and those that would not. This would include a view of:
the people that would transfer with the separated businesses and the people that support each business but would not transfer;
the processes that would transfer with the separated businesses and the processes leveraged by each business would not transfer;
the IT systems that would transfer with the separated businesses and the IT systems shared with each business but would not transfer;
the facilities, equipment, and assets that would transfer with the separated businesses and the facilities shared with each business but would not transfer; and
the contracts, licenses, and agreements that would transfer with the separated businesses and the contracts leveraged by each business would not transfer.
Standalone Target Operating Model (TOM)

Based on the Separation Perimeter and leveraging our standard framework, develop a document that provides a clear, detailed view of how the Target would operate as a self-sufficient standalone operation free of any engagements, dependencies, or support form a parent or sister organization by functional area (Operations, Procurement, Sales, HR, IT, Finance, etc.). This will show any incremental people, facilities, tools, asset, or agreements that would be required to achieve such a standalone state.
Standalone costs

Reviewing the carve-out perimeter, identify operational gaps to operate the standalone asset and quantify. Standalone costs will be determined through the triangulation of the following three methods to produce an estimate of the standalone cost:
Historical financial dependencies
Perform a detailed review of corporate recharges and cost allocations, identifying services recharged at cost and services recharged under allocation mechanisms (e.g. cost plus, fixed costs, etc.);
Review historical trending of costs for the carve-out and (if possible) the parent; and
Cross reference financial dependencies against operational dependencies to identify services provided at no cost and artificial recharges incurred.
Benchmarking

Perform external functional benchmarking of other players in the industry and related industries from publicly available information, KPMG’s previous engagements and from the KPMG proprietary Database;
Bottom up review
Build up costs, bottom up, based on operational KPI’s, complemented by a top-down assessment to estimate standalone costs, dis-synergies and diseconomies of scale in the following areas:
Executive / senior team including stock-based compensation and LTIP;
Back office / G&A FTE and non-FTE (HR, Finance, Legal, etc.);
Supply agreements;
Technology – systems & infrastructure , including proprietary systems;
Outsourced providers;
Head office costs; and
Sourcing and procurement for front store goods & indirect costs.
One-time costs. Determine the potential one-time costs which may be incurred by the buyer
Separation risks
Identify key implementation considerations for the integration and potential challenges to the separation, including IT.
Identify material risks associated with the deal, separation, and transition to your desired operating model state.
Produce KPMG Assessment
Provide overall summary and supporting report of KPMG’s view of separation considerations and risks alongside an estimate of standalone costs;
Propose a range of adjustments to the Quality of Earnings, based on our detailed review; and
Highlight additional key risks and upsides.
TSA Support

We assume Seller will provide a comprehensive TSA consisting of a Front-end (containing the legal and commercial terms and conditions), a series of Schedules (containing the detailed descriptions of the various services to be provided), any accompanying Exhibits to the schedules, and indication of fees/costs for each of the services.
Based on the Day 1 Operating Model and our previous experience with similar transactions, assist management to:

TSA Front-end – review and provide comments on the Seller’s proposed TSA terms and conditions for key commercial terms such as duration, termination rights, extension rights, service levels, dispute resolution mechanisms, change request processes, etc., to ensure that the agreement in fair and contains market terms;

TSA Schedules and Exhibits – review and provide comments on the Seller’s proposed TSA schedule of services for each service area, including details around scope inclusions, scope exclusions, dependencies, duration, extensions, service levels etc. to provide Buy-side balance and appropriate coverage during the transition;
TSA costing – review the Seller’s proposed TSA fee costing and structure to ensure it is within bounds of reasonability, through:
Comparison to bottom-up standalone cost build and historical allocation
Understanding of TSA durations
Determining the reasonability of fixed and variable nature costs;
Understanding of parallel costs incurred to stand-up function during the TSA period;
One-time costs required to stand-up and terminate functions; and
Develop a monthly view from post-Close to expected end state

TSA negotiations – help management identify potential areas of risk and weakness and suggest potential mediations. Work with your internal/external Legal counsels and/or operational team(s) to review and mark-up any of the TSA documents as requested, participate in review or plenary meetings regarding the TSA, and participate in any negotiations with Seller as requested by you.
Phase 2: Integration Execution

The following assumes that Management has articulated a clear deal thesis, has developed a clear strategy for the integration of the assets, and can produce a fully developed and detailed synergy model with quantities and timing by effort/project.
Day 1 Readiness (4 – 6 weeks)

Integration Management Office (IMO) setup and mobilization: Help management set up the IMO, mobilize the governance structure, and reporting tools and cadences required to run the integration program.

Hotspots assessment: Based on document reviews and potential interviews with key management team members, develop a documented assessment that will identify the key integration issues by functional area under key five key headings: people, processes, systems, facilities, and contracts. This will help identify the major issues are risks that need to be planned for and mitigated.

Day 1 Target Operating Model (TOM) development: Work with management to develop a detailed TOM and produce a comprehensive listing of categories of responsibilities by functional area to indicate those elements of the acquired operations that will:
transfer with the transaction;
not transfer but covered under Transition Services Agreement (TSA);
not transfer but may need to be covered under Long Term Agreement (LTA); and
not transfer and not be covered under TSA/LTA, so therefore need to be replaced by deal close
This target state will serve as the basis for the transition plans.
Integration Blueprint: help Management develop a document that establishes:
The vision, principles, goals, timelines, governance and priorities for the integration; and

The Day 1 and transitional Operating Models which outline by functional area those processes and responsibilities that will be integrated into the SDM business along with the time horizons for the various transitions.
Communications and change management plans required to support the envisioned transitions and timelines.

Facilitated Team Kickoff session: Leveraging the content developed above, conduct a half-day facilitated session designed to bring the two teams together and mobilize the combined integration team for the merger.

Detailed Day 1 plan development: Help each functional area/workstream prepare detailed Day 1 plan a full set of consolidated integration plans, leveraging what already exists across the functional areas and augmenting with our previous integration experience as appropriate.

Facilitate a Day 1 Dependency Workshop session: Similar to the Team Kickoff session above, design and conduct a half-day facilitated session designed to engage with the core project team to surface inter-functional dependencies of their Day 1 plans via a “Speed Dating” exercise.

Pressure Testing Workshop: Design and conduct a stakeholder-based simulation of the Day1 state by doing a walk-through of the key processes “in the shoes” of the stakeholder(s) in order to test and build confidence in the Day 1 Workplans.

Cutover Walk Through: A facilitated workshop, ideally with the Target, to walk through sequencing of tasks and events being completed across all workstreams leading up to the deal close, Tasks to be extracted from the workplans and sequenced based on timing.

Day 1 Command Centre: Support the planning, setup, and communication of the Day 1 Command Centre, which will serve as a centralized hub for nay issues that may arise for a defined period of time after close.
Post-Close PMO Support (6 – 8 weeks)

Integration Management Office (IMO) operations: Continue the day-to-day operation of the IMO, using the tools, processes, and reporting already established as well as introducing new ones as required. This includes aligning leadership, preparing for and facilitating workshops, and any other facilitated team initiatives required for the integration. In addition, we will provide direct hands-on integration and project management support to Workstreams as required.
Day 100 Planning: Work with workstreams and functional areas to develop detailed plans for initiatives to occur int eh first 100 days of post-close at the workstream level.

End-state Target Operating Model (TOM): A document that provides a clear, detailed outline of the transitional and final state operating model options for the entity. The TOM will provide a detailed view of the people, processes, facilities, and technologies broken down by functional area and geography, indicating which aspects will remain as is, which will need to be replaced immediately, and which can be changed over time (including an indication of expected time horizon).

Integration project charters: Based on management’s synergy and one-time cost estimates, support each of the Workstreams to identify the set of key integration projects that will drive the merger and develop appropriate set of project Charters that include description, objectives, and scope, and has an allocated synergy target and/or one-time spend budget. Each Charter should further break down the allocations into actionable projects with, clear objectives, high level timelines, and indicative milestones.

Synergy and One-time Cost tracking: Assist management to establish and deploy appropriate tools and processes to track and report on synergies and one-time cost throughout the integration program. This assumes Synergies and One-time Costs targets have been established and do not require further refinement or evaluation. We are happy to discuss alternate approaches if this assumption does not hold true.
Project progress tracking and reporting: Develop Steering Committee/Executive/Ownership update presentations and updates as required.

Training and Handover: As required, work with your designate(s) to transition integration plans, including risk mitigation activities, for execution, positioning the execution team for self-sufficiency.
